EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly warm and moist system brought accumulating snow to the higher terrain and rain mixed with freezing rain to the lower valleys of western Montana. Total snow amounts ranged between 7 to 12 inches.  This system carried over from December 31st, 2009.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dynamic storm system moved out of Southern Canada and across the Billings Forecast Area on the 5th and 6th of January. Ahead of the system, cold arctic air surged southward across the area. Moist air forced over this arctic air resulted in periods of heavy snowfall. In addition, gusty northerly winds created considerable blowing and drifting snow along with bitterly cold wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 5 inches were reported from Harlowton to Lavina with very bad road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and gusty winds impacted portions of northwest and southwest Montana in both the valleys and mountains. Total snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches in the valleys to over a foot in the mountains in northwest Montana. Gusty north winds caused blowing and drifting of snow which created dangerous driving conditions on roadways and caused local schools to close in Flathead County. Power outages were also reported in Flathead County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported by weather observers ranging from 8 to 11 inches in the valleys. Big Mountain Ski Resort received 13 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period.

Power outages were reported throughout the county from downed power lines. Total snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous shortwave and associated cold front produced very strong winds in portions of western Montana on April 8th.  Wind speeds up to 60 mph were reported with this system.  Damage to trees, powerlines, cars and property occured virtually area wide.

A tree fell on a house on Sky Road in Whitefish.  Observations in the area measured winds between 40 to 50 mph sustained with estimated gusts up to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low pressure system moving through western Montana and north central Idaho push a strong jet stream and associated cold front through the region. Snow amounts ranged from five to 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Georgetown reported six inches of snow.  A trained spotter seven miles south of Butte reported seven inches of heavy snow. The Butte DOT reported six to eight inches of snow over Homestake and MacDonald Passes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low deepened over central and eastern Montana with wrap around moisture affecting western Montana.  The main impact was to Glacier National Park where eight to nine inches of snow fell in the lower elevations, and up to 42 inches of snow was reported above 6000 feet.  In elevations below 4000 feet, impressive rain fall amounts were recorded, ranging from 0.60 to 4.4 of liquid.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various SNOTELs in the area reported anywhere from seven to 42 inches of snow above 4000 feet.  Severe driving conditions were declared over Marias Pass due to snow and visibility issues by the Montana DOT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 59 mph was reported at Three Forks 1SW (the Three Forks AWOS site). A wind gust to 69 mph was also reported at Belgrade 1NE (the Bozeman ASOS site).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability and increasing flow aloft combined with an upper low and moisture over north central Oregon produced favorable conditions for severe thunderstorms across the northern Rockies. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter sized hail in the Lolo Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system from the Gulf of Alaska moved through the region, pushing a strong jet stream and cold front into the northern Rockies. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and fairly cold fast moving trough from the Gulf of Alaska with an associated strong cold front moved through the region. The speed of the trough and surface pressure gradient orientation matched the composite map types for high wind events. A very strong 150 knot jet was associated with the trough and was digging into southern Idaho. Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred throughout the region with areas of damage from tree blow downs, and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Seeley Lake measured wind gusts up to 71 mph.  Numerous trees were reported down, some up to one foot in diameter.  Trees were blown down on to the power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad and cold upper level trough of low pressure moved over the northern Rockies, providing moisture to most locations across western Montana and north central Idaho. Snow levels lowered enough to impact mountain passes with accumulating snow. Total amounts ranged between four to seven inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Divide reported seven inches of new snow.  A trained spotter five miles southwest of Butte reported nine inches of new snow.  Five of those inches fell in about three hours.

In one case, the thunderstorm sat stationary for an hour and dumped heavy rain over Hot Springs.  This led to flash flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car traveling over Logan Pass sustained dents from the hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS storm survey team ran across a pile of hail 15 hours after the hail event occured which was still 0.75 inches in diameter.  Hail was likely quite a bit larger when it initially fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dynamic storm system moved out of Southern Canada and across the Billings Forecast Area on the 5th and 6th of January. Ahead of the system, cold arctic air surged southward across the area. Moist air forced over this arctic air resulted in periods of heavy snowfall. In addition, gusty northerly winds created considerable blowing and drifting snow along with bitterly cold wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Forsyth received 6 inches of snow along with significant drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable and moist atmosphere led to another round of severe thunderstorms across portions of western Montana.  The main threat with these storms was hail up to 1.25 inches and heavy rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator reported a wind gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pacific storm and an arctic cold front clashed over northeast Montana on December 29th to bring heavy snow to much of the area that continued into the night. North winds and and an arctic airmass on the backside of the system brought blizzard conditions in some places and dangerous windchills late on the 29th that lasted into the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1346 MST on December 29th, Wolf Point ASOS reported blizzard conditions with sustained winds greater than 25 mph and frequent gusts over 35 mph between 1235 and 1830 MST. At 1714 MST on December 29th, 8 miles southwest of Bredette, spotter reported blizzard conditions with sustained winds of 25 mph or greater with gusts over 35 mph. The peak gust was 41 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front dropped south across the area early in the day with a Pacific disturbance overrunning moisture up and over the frontal boundary to bring a west to east band of snowfall across the area. 6 to 8 inches of snow in a narrow band fell from central Phillips east along Highway 2 to eastern Roosevelt county. Strong easterly winds created significant impacts on east/west roadways. Severe driving conditions and automobile accidents occurred. Many school districts in Valley and Roosevelt counties dismissed students early with afternoon and evening events being cancelled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 6 to 10 inches were measured with this storm. Official NWS observation was 6.5 inches at the Glasgow Airport. There were also reports of drifting to about 30 inches deep.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fisher Creek Snotel reported 17 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized freezing rain caused some schools to delay opening and closed a portion of I-90 in west central Montana on 1/12/2010.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused very slick roadways and closed a portion of I-90 from near Ninemile to the Y. Some Bitterroot and Missoula Valley schools were running two hours late due to bus delays. The Missoula International Airport closed down briefly due to freezing rain on runways. Twenty car crashes were reported along I-90 and in the town of Missoula during the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep surface low pressure system moved across northern Montana and brought cold temperatures and widespread snow. Snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell at Bozeman. Also, 22 inches of snow fell at West Yellowstone 6S (the Madison Plateau SNOTEL site - elevation 7750 feet). Fourteen inches of snow fell at West Yellowstone 11S (the Black Bear SNOTEL site - elevation 7949 feet). Eight inches of snow fell at West Yellowstone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow fell at Rocky Boy 8SE (the Rocky Boy SNOTEL site - elevation 4700 feet). Eight inches of snow fell at Havre.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mild and moist Pacific airmass moved up and over a colder airmass at the surface. Temperatures in many areas were below freezing where the rainfall occurred. Automobile accidents and road closures occurred in many areas in Valley, Roosevelt, Richland and Sheridan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported icy roadways with a quarter of an inch ice accumulation. Numerous traffic accidents occurred.  The airport also had canceled flights and significant delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, moist subtropical jet stream overran an arctic front to bring a west to east strip of heavy snow across northeast Montana. Amounts close to a foot were common from Glasgow east to Richland county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 6 inches of snow fell across the area with greatest amounts likely to have occurred in the central and eastern portions of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, moist subtropical jet stream overran an arctic front to bring a west to east strip of heavy snow across northeast Montana. Amounts close to a foot were common from Glasgow east to Richland county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of up to 9 inches were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mild and moist Pacific airmass moved up and over a colder airmass at the surface. Temperatures in many areas were below freezing where the rainfall occurred. Automobile accidents and road closures occurred in many areas in Valley, Roosevelt, Richland and Sheridan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain of less than a tenth of an inch occurred that resulted in slick roadways and traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure moving across southern Canada contributed to strong winds developing along the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 80 mph was reported at Bynum 13W. Also, a gust to 66 mph was reported at Dupuyer 9WSW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system brought moisture to North Central Montana where it interacted with cold air dropping south out of Canada. The resulting snow produced accumulations in excess of 6 inches at many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at West Yellowstone 24NW (the Beaver Creek SNOTEL site - elevation 7850 feet).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported nearly a quarter inch of ice accumulation. Numerous traffic accidents occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at Geyser 2SE.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at Lewistown. Also, 8 inches of snow fell at Lewistown 18SSW (the Crystal Lake SNOTEL site - elevation 6050 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep surface low pressure system moved across northern Montana and brought cold temperatures and widespread snow. Snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nineteen inches of snow fell at Millegan 14SE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mild and moist Pacific airmass moved up and over a colder airmass at the surface. Temperatures in many areas were below freezing where the rainfall occurred. Automobile accidents and road closures occurred in many areas in Valley, Roosevelt, Richland and Sheridan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain of a tenth of an inch to two tenths of an inch fell across the area resulting in slick roadways and traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front dropped south across the area early in the day with a Pacific disturbance overrunning moisture up and over the frontal boundary to bring a west to east band of snowfall across the area. 6 to 8 inches of snow in a narrow band fell from central Phillips east along Highway 2 to eastern Roosevelt county. Strong easterly winds created significant impacts on east/west roadways. Severe driving conditions and automobile accidents occurred. Many school districts in Valley and Roosevelt counties dismissed students early with afternoon and evening events being cancelled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bainville through Brockton reported snowfall with visibilities of a quarter of a mile or less in heavy snow with blowing and drifting. Snowfall amounts were in the 2 to 4 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with an intense low pressure system produced very strong and gusty winds ahead of and with the fronts passage. Strong winds were reported near Florence in Ravalli county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm wind gusts 3 miles east of Florence in Ravalli county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold arctic air moving out of Canada combined with a moist pacific storm system. This produced blizzard conditions across much of western Montana, along with very cold wind chill temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured 7 inches of snow 4 miles ne of Ronan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold arctic air moving out of Canada combined with a moist pacific storm system. This produced blizzard conditions across much of western Montana, along with very cold wind chill temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDOT weather observation reported wind chill readings of 25 below to 30 below zero from 1 pm to 6 pm at Powell Mt.  ASOS at Bert Mooney airport in Butte reported a wind chill temperature of 28 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved from southern California into the northern Rockies and northern Plains.  Very stable conditions ahead of the storm system created several days of very dense fog from the 18th through the 22nd of January.  This fog created significant delays of commercial and general aviation flights, as well as emergency medical flights having to be grounded.  The freezing fog and rime ice, and some light freezing rain accumulated on power lines, causing the lines to snap starting on the 20th and 21st of January.  As the storm approached, snow quickly spread from south to north across the area on the afternoon of the 22nd.  When the main cold front finally moved through late on the 22nd and the 23rd, the winds switched from the east to the north and northwest with speeds from 25 to 40 mph, resulting in blizzard conditions.  Widespread road closures and whiteout conditions kept power crews from being able to restore power, and many residents were without power until January 25th and 26th. Snowfall measurements were difficult to obtain east of Valley and Garfield counties due to the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State DES reported there was extensive electrical pole damage across the area due to rime ice accumulation. Dollar amounts were from NorVal and Sheridan Electric.  Commercial and general aviation interests were also impacted with flights unable to come in or fly out of the Wolf Point and Poplar Airports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving weather system brought snow to portions of the Billings forecast area beginning late on the evening of January 31st and continued through the day on February 1st. Travelers during the morning commute on the 1st encountered snow covered and slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas in and around Lavina received anywhere from 5 to 7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter 1 SSE Zortman estimated 15 inches of snowfall. Wind gusts over 40 mph resulted in blizzard conditions and snow drifts of six to eight feet. The wind started on the evening of the 23rd and lasted through the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall estimates ranged from seven to eight inches in most locations before the wind hit the area. 14 inches were estimated 5 SW of Hinsdale the morning of the 24th. Sustained winds over 30 mph created blizzard conditions across the entire area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts over 40 mph along with new snowfall created blizzard conditions on the morning of January 24th. The blizzard conditions lasted through the morning of January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated seven inches of new snowfall along with drifting snow. Wind gusts over 35 mph created blizzard conditions on the morning of January 24th which lasted until into the next morniing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of blizzard conditions with wind gusts over 45 mph, sustained winds over 35 mph and visibilities less than a quarter of a mile. Blizzard conditions started the morning of the January 24th and lasted through the morning of the January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds brought a brief period of high winds to a couple locations across the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 46 mph with a gust to 61 mph was recorded at the Judith Gap DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds over 40 mph were observed (McDonalds DOT). widespread blizzard conditions were reported in Wolf Point with observed wind gusts to 40 mph. Reports of sustained winds over 35 mph were reported throughout the day at Poplar on January 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stateline DOT observations reported sustained winds of 30 mph with gusts over 40 mph. The blizzard conditions started the morning of January 24th and lasted into the afternoon of January 25th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters estimated six to eight inches of snow. Ground blizzards were also observed and the Winnett observations reported gusts in the upper 40s. These conditions came to an end on the afternoon of January 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported eight to ten inches of snow. Blowing snow reduced visibility to less than a quarter of a mile at times. Some roads were drifted shut. Wind gusts to 48 mph were reported with roads in and around Jordan closed. Visibilities were less than a quarter of a mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were reported as gusts were observed up to 46 mph. Near Brockway, visibility never got above a quarter of a mile with drifts of 10 feet. The county opened up a Red Cross shelter as people had been stuck on county roadways. Power poles were also damaged. Dollar amounts are estimated from actual losses, not to replace them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions started the morning of January 24th with snowfall and high winds. The blizzard conditions lasted until the afternoon of January 25th. Reporting stations registered wind gusts over 35 mph lasting over 24 hours (Sioux Pass DOT sensor).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions began with sustained winds between 30 and 40 mph, lasting over 24 hours in places. Six to eight inches of snowfall were commonly reported across the zone. Blowing snow reduced the visibility to less than a quarter of a mile 35 S of Malta and across other parts of the zone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated new snowfall of 6 inches with some blowing and drifting. Winds were sustained at 30 to 40 mph creating blizzard conditions. which started on the morning of January 24th and lasting until the early morning on January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.

Lindsay Divide DOT reported sustained winds over 50 mph with gusts over 60 mph while blizzard conditions lasted more than 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions started the morning of January 24th with snowfall and high winds. The blizzard conditions lasted until the late morning of January 25th. Spotter reported visibility less than 50 yards all day with some neighbors without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling and/or loose snow on the ground to create large drifts and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was around zero all day in Wibaux, which caused semi trucks to be stuck on Interstate 94. One snow drift was measured at 56 inches deep. Gusts over 35 mph lasted for over 24 hours (Beaver Hill DOT).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across Southern Montana late on the 13th. As a result, snow fell across all but the far western areas of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Absarokee received 6 inches of snow while Park City received 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Billings Forecast Area during the afternoon and evening of the 17th. This combined with an upper level disturbance brought heavy snow to the Beartooth/Absaroka Foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific flow brought heavy snow to the Beartooth and Absaroka Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fisher Creek and White Mill Snotels received 18 and 11 inches of snow respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system developed over the Central Rockies and allowed a cold air mass to drop south out of Canada. The system was followed by a very moist and strong westerly flow aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roundup area received between 8 and 10 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved from southern California into the northern Rockies and northern Plains.  Very stable conditions ahead of the storm system created several days of very dense fog from the 18th through the 22nd of January.  This fog created significant delays of commercial and general aviation flights, as well as emergency medical flights having to be grounded.  The freezing fog and rime ice, and some light freezing rain accumulated on power lines, causing the lines to snap starting on the 20th and 21st of January.  As the storm approached, snow quickly spread from south to north across the area on the afternoon of the 22nd.  When the main cold front finally moved through late on the 22nd and the 23rd, the winds switched from the east to the north and northwest with speeds from 25 to 40 mph, resulting in blizzard conditions.  Widespread road closures and whiteout conditions kept power crews from being able to restore power, and many residents were without power until January 25th and 26th. Snowfall measurements were difficult to obtain east of Valley and Garfield counties due to the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter 1 SSE Zortman estimated 15 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system developed over the Central Rockies and allowed a cold air mass to drop south out of Canada. The system was followed by a very moist and strong westerly flow aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 7 inches was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm developed over the Central Rockies. As the storm slowly moved northeast into the Northern High Plains, it brought heavy snow to Southern Montana. In addition, strong northwest winds and heavy snow resulted in blizzard conditions to portions of far Eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 12 was closed east of Miles City due to snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough off the Pacific Northwest brought a very moist westerly flow aloft across the area. Significant snowfall occurred over a two day period across the western portions of the Billings forecast Area...mainly over the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The eastern areas of Park County received anywhere from 10 to 18 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough brought a deep and moist southerly flow to the area. Synoptic lift and jet dynamics affected the western zones first. Temperatures were warm enough in the Beartooth Mountains to prevent significant accumulations but warning criteria was still met. Most of the system focused on the far east as a very narrow, stationary, mid level convergent area developed through the day and evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of heavy snow producing 5 to 11 inches occurred across portions of Fallon County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tight pressure gradients across the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountain Foothills resulted in high winds across the area. This resulted in the recent fresh snow cover to significantly blow and drift...especially in the Livingston and Nye areas. Visibilities near zero at times combined with large snow drifts resulted in I-90 in the Livingston area being closed for nearly two days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ground Blizzard conditions with near zero visibilities and drifting snow occurred on Highway 191.

Icy and snow covered roads were common during the late night and early morning hours of the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadus and Volborg received 7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous shortwave and associated cold front produced very strong winds in portions of western Montana on April 8th.  Wind speeds up to 60 mph were reported with this system.  Damage to trees, powerlines, cars and property occured virtually area wide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility company construction site measured winds gusting up to 60 mph.  The same utility company reported scattered power outages from trees down on power lines through Silver Bow County and the Butte area.  Also, a tree fell on a vehicle in the Butte jail parking lot.  A Ford F-150 sustained major damage to its windshield.  Severe driving conditions were reported over MacDonald Pass by the Department of Transportation due to poor visibility in blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and slow moving Spring storm system moved across Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming. This storm brought heavy snow to the Crazy, and Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains, as well as the higher terrain of Southern Big Horn County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Fork Shields Snotel received 27 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep surface low pressure moving across southern Canada produced a tight surface gradient across North Central Montana which resulted in high winds. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common. Many trees were downed by the winds as were power poles resulting in thousands of customers losing service.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 60 mph was reported at Canyon Creek 7SE. A gust to 60 mph was also reported at Lincoln 3E. High winds toppled a semi-trailer on Interstate 15 near the South Helena Interchange. Many trees were also reported blown down in Helena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms produced large hail and heavy rainfall. The areas affected were in southern Valley County, Richland, Dawson and Priairie counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding washed out low spot on County Road 120.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms produced large hail and heavy rainfall. The areas affected were in southern Valley County, Richland, Dawson and Priairie counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.07 inches fell from a thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A law enforcement officer reported one inch hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Richland County Law enforcement reported a washed out low spot on County Highway 120 about two miles west of Highway 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms produced large hail and heavy rainfall. The areas affected were in southern Valley County, Richland, Dawson and Priairie counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain closed the Berry Street underpass with six feet of water. Highway 16 near milemarker 8 also experienced water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep surface low pressure moving across southern Canada produced a tight surface gradient across North Central Montana which resulted in high winds. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common. Many trees were downed by the winds as were power poles resulting in thousands of customers losing service.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 63 mph was reported at Townsend 9W (the Elkhorn RAWS site). A wind gust to 75 mph was also reported at Townsend 6NNW. The location is at the south end of Canyon Ferry Reservoir where a number of trees were reported downed. Trees 8 to 10 inches in diameter and 25 to 35 feet tall were blown over. Winds also tore the roofs off of two buildings near Townsend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and fairly cold fast moving trough from the Gulf of Alaska with an associated strong cold front moved through the region. The speed of the trough and surface pressure gradient orientation matched the composite map types for high wind events. A very strong 150 knot jet was associated with the trough and was digging into southern Idaho. Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred throughout the region with areas of damage from tree blow downs, and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was blown over on to a vehicle at Mullan Road and Grassland Drive in Missoula.  A semi truck was overturned between mile markers 78 and 79 on Highway 93 south.  Several power poles were snapped in that area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on the 24th and 25th caused widespread low land flooding across rural Custer County, mainly south of Miles City.  Heavy rainfall also caused numerous roads and culverts to wash out in Custer County. A five day total of 4.08 inches at Volborg was reported on the 25th . On the morning of May 25th, a cooperative observer reported that Pumpkin Creek was out of its banks 5 miles north of Volborg with low land flooding. In addition, runoff from recent heavy rains also caused water levels to rise on the Tongue River below the Twelve Mile Dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on the 24th and 25th caused widespread low land flooding across rural Custer County, mainly south of Miles City.  Heavy rainfall also caused numerous roads and culverts to wash out in Custer County. A five day total of 4.08 inches at Volborg was reported on the 25th . On the morning of May 25th, a cooperative observer reported that Pumpkin Creek was out of its banks 5 miles north of Volborg with low land flooding. In addition, runoff from recent heavy rains also caused water levels to rise on the Tongue River below the Twelve Mile Dam.

Smaller creeks flooded and Highway 566 was closed from Birney to Ashland due to water and debris running over the road. Road damage was reported throughout Custer County.

Montana State University reported up to 1000 windows damaged on campus with a repair estimate in excess of $100,000. The city of Bozeman also reported cleaning up nearly 5 tons of debris consisting of tress and tree limbs. State Farm representatives estimate that Gallatin Valley residents will submit a total of $60 million in claims resulting from the storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph with gusts over 58 mph were reported at the Billings airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather continued into the third week of June across the Billings Forecast Area. |Plenty of low-level moisture continued to be in place across Southeast Montana. Strong winds aloft along, an upper level disturbance and low-level moisture all combined to help produce numerous thunderstorms across Southeast Montana. A tornado watch was issued for the afternoon and evening hours. Numerous severe thunderstorms developed by mid afternoon and continued well into the evening hours. A couple thunderstorms produced tornadoes, one of which was the Cache Creek Tornado.

Flooding occurred behind the culvert on the Billingsley Road, which is located 9 miles west-northwest of Glasgow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two sets of storm chasers reported a rope tornado that touched down sometime between 854 and 859 pm MDT. There were some conflicts between reports on timing and exact location. One report had the tornado on the ground for 15 seconds.

At 1005 MST, Phillips County dispatch reported that a camper was blown over on Highway 191. High winds snapped a pine tree in Malta. 5 miles east of Malta, the high winds wrapped trampoline around a tree. The high winds also caused a phone outage in Malta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere had warmed and dried from the first 90 degree day of the summer on Monday June 28. By Tuesday June 29, very unstable air associated with increasing southwesterly flow and moisture aloft provided the ingredients for a severe weather event.  A strong shortwave associated with an approaching jet stream approached the area. The first severe storm developed Tuesday afternoon over Ovando in Powell County with wind gusts up to 60 mph blowing trees down. By early evening, the transition had already taken place for large hail producers as large hail was noted first near Condon. Two severe storms passed near Missoula and Lolo at 940 PM and again at 11 PM  with large hail and wind. The most significant severe weather occurred between Stevensville and Florence, as a line of storms produced outflow damaging winds estimated at 88 to 100 mph between 1045 PM to 1130 PM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee at Trixi's Bar in Ovando estimated nickel size hail and heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest flow aloft coupled with a weak jet stream brought vast amounts of moisture and energy over the northern Rockies.  A few severe thunderstorms formed across southwest Montana that produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sheriffs office in Deer Lodge received one inch diameter hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest flow aloft coupled with a weak jet stream brought vast amounts of moisture and energy over the northern Rockies.  A few severe thunderstorms formed across southwest Montana that produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail estimated by the COOP observer in Georgetown Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere had warmed and dried from the first 90 degree day of the summer on Monday June 28. By Tuesday June 29, very unstable air associated with increasing southwesterly flow and moisture aloft provided the ingredients for a severe weather event.  A strong shortwave associated with an approaching jet stream approached the area. The first severe storm developed Tuesday afternoon over Ovando in Powell County with wind gusts up to 60 mph blowing trees down. By early evening, the transition had already taken place for large hail producers as large hail was noted first near Condon. Two severe storms passed near Missoula and Lolo at 940 PM and again at 11 PM  with large hail and wind. The most significant severe weather occurred between Stevensville and Florence, as a line of storms produced outflow damaging winds estimated at 88 to 100 mph between 1045 PM to 1130 PM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail 1.25 inches in diameter was reported at Lindbergh Lake.  Small branches were stripped off pine trees and were covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest flow aloft coupled with a weak jet stream brought vast amounts of moisture and energy over the northern Rockies.  A few severe thunderstorms formed across southwest Montana that produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter sized hail coming down hard.  He also received 0.35 inches of rain in five minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter (located 1 mile north of the Flowing Wells Rest Area) reported 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moving northeast into southern Saskatchewan allowed for strong west to northwest winds to blow across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fort Peck Handar reported sustained winds of 40 mph or more for more than two hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Sawmill RAWS station reported 65 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast across the area helping to drive northwest winds across the area behind it. Heavy rain also occurred in areas with the passage of the low pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 2000 MST power lines and trees were knocked down in the Poplar area. At 2058 MST Poplar RAWS recorded a wind gust of 59 mph, also McDonalds DOT recorded a wind gust of 61 mph. At 2118 MST Poplar RAWS recorded a sustained wind of 49 mph with a gust to 59 mph, at 444 MST McDonalds DOT reported a 70 mph wind gust, at 505 MST McDonalds DOT recorded a wind gust of 79 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and fairly cold fast moving trough from the Gulf of Alaska with an associated strong cold front moved through the region. The speed of the trough and surface pressure gradient orientation matched the composite map types for high wind events. A very strong 150 knot jet was associated with the trough and was digging into southern Idaho. Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred throughout the region with areas of damage from tree blow downs, and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a house and in the power lines in Butte.  In Anaconda, a tree broke in half at Main and Front Streets.  The fallen tree broke the top of a light pole and also blocked the street.  In addition, a porch blew off a house in Anaconda sending broken glass on to Chestnut Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong and deep surface low moved across the Northern Rockies and Southern Canada on the 3rd and 4th. As a result of very tight pressure gradients across Central and Eastern Montana, very strong sustained and gusty winds were reported across the entire Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph with gusts over 58 mph were reported at the Hillside DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved east across southern Saskatchewan. High winds associated with a trailing cold front helped to kick up brush fires in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least five fires were reported, with one caused by power lines down south/southeast of Glasgow.  One barn was destroyed. The fires were at the Aitken Farm, Sullivan Park, near Brazil Creek, north of Frazer, and an old pit burn from a month ago south of Glentana re-erupted into sparks and created another fire. The main fires started around 1730 and final crews left scene/mop up around 4 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Meteorologically speaking, this was a very active severe weather day for south central Montana. A very moist and unstable atmosphere was in place across portions of the Billings Forecast area during the afternoon and evening of the 20th (Father's Day). A moist, southeast surface flow, strong wind shear aloft, and ample afternoon heating provided the necessary ingredients for severe weather. Numerous thunderstorms, some of which became rapidly severe producing tornadoes and large hail, developed across South Central Montana.

A strong system ejecting out of the central Rockies brought heavy rainfall and severe thunderstorms to the area during the evening. This episode produced an EF1 tornado in northern McCone county and a microburst in eastern Roosevelt county that killed one person near Froid, MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall occurred in southern Wibaux and northern Fallon County. Reports of rainfall up to 5 in this area were received by NWS Billings spotters.   This water flowed into the Beaver Creek, which flows northward into the town of Wibaux.   It was out of it's banks in many places by the morning of June 17th.  No major damage was caused.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Landowner reported a tornado that snapped numerous trees and damaged some metal storage sheds across his property. Tornadic winds were estimated from NWS Survey at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough extending from the Pacific Northwest to the Great Basin provided a deep southerly flow aloft over the area. This allowed thunderstorms over western Garfield, southern Phillips and southwest Valley counties to contain a great deal of moisture. Heavy rain and hail accompanied these storms as they mainly tracked northwest and occurred in remote locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain estimated at several inches (radar estimated at five inches) fell mainly in the Second and Third Creek basins in the southern portion of the county resulting in runoff over a roadway (Dry Fork Road). This runoff began during the evening of the 18th and continued into the morning of the 19th. This is only the second time that this has happened in the past twelve years (according to the Trained Spotter). The damaged section of road is about �� mile east of the Dry Fork Bridge. The force of the runoff carried away a portion of the south shoulder of the road; creating an abrupt drop off about 75 feet long and 3 feet deep.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms across the region had widespread reports of winds in the 60 to 75 mph range, but a microburst 3 miles west of the town of Froid produced winds estimated based off of the damage survey at 110 to 125 mph.  The winds hit just after 9 pm MDT.   A modular home that was strapped to the ground had the straps snapped, and it was lifted from its foundation, thrown 30 feet to the north-northeast and flipped over.   Two people were in the home at the time and a female ended up approximately 250-280 feet north-northeast of the main debris area.  The female victim died shortly afterwards and the male victim was hospitalized with injuries including a broken arm and collarbone.  The home was a total loss.  A garage next to the home had damage to the garage doors, the siding on the west side of the home, and some of the roofing shingles torn off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Meteorologically speaking, this was a very active severe weather day for south central Montana. A very moist and unstable atmosphere was in place across portions of the Billings Forecast area during the afternoon and evening of the 20th (Father's Day). A moist, southeast surface flow, strong wind shear aloft, and ample afternoon heating provided the necessary ingredients for severe weather. Numerous thunderstorms, some of which became rapidly severe producing tornadoes and large hail, developed across South Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on the observed damage, the tornado was classified as an EF-2 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale. Wind speeds within an EF-2 tornado range from 111-135 mph, and the associated damage observed at the Billings MetraPark and nearby businesses was consistent with this classification. The damage path was 120 yards wide with a length of about a half mile and on the ground an estimated 12 minutes.||The damage assessment and eyewitness accounts indicate that the tornado developed near the intersection of Lake Elmo Drive and Main Street in the Billings Heights at approximately 4:24 pm, with significant EF-2 damage to several nearby businesses. Damage included rooftops being blown off of three structures, windows blown out, power poles downed, business signs and billboards blown down along with several trees uprooted. The tornado appeared to weaken slightly as it progressed southeast across Alkali Creek. Limbs were broken off numerous trees in the vicinity of the creek. The tornadic circulation then appeared to have strengthened once again as it moved south over the Rimrock Auto Arena at Metrapark. EF-2 damage was again observed to the arena with much of the roof blown off along with other damage to the exterior of the building. Debris from the arena impacted other nearby businesses creating additional damage, mainly in the form of broken windows. Debris from the arena was reported landing as far away as a mile from the tornado touchdown. The tornado then dissipated over the arena around 4:36 pm.||The associated thunderstorm then moved northeast away from Billings. Numerous sightings of funnel clouds were reported as this storm moved east-northeast of Billings, however no additional tornado touchdowns were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Meteorologically speaking, this was a very active severe weather day for south central Montana. A very moist and unstable atmosphere was in place across portions of the Billings Forecast area during the afternoon and evening of the 20th (Father's Day). A moist, southeast surface flow, strong wind shear aloft, and ample afternoon heating provided the necessary ingredients for severe weather. Numerous thunderstorms, some of which became rapidly severe producing tornadoes and large hail, developed across South Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters from a thunderstorm turned downtown streets into rivers, parking lots into lakes and basements into swamps. So much rain fell so fast on Billings that storm drains wide enough to hold a small car were filled for hours. Streets turned into rushing rivers, basements filled with muddy water and some residents went swimming with shovels and sticks to clear clogged storm drains. More than an inch of water seeped into the Billings Clinic hospital corridors and the basement near the buildings north entrances. Mud and water flooded the atrium area of Billings Clinic. In the hospital's north parking lot, storm water rose above the tires of passenger cars. Flooding was also reported in the underground parking garage of the hospital. Flash flooding was also reported on Alkali Creek as the creek flooded in several places. There were numerous calls of flooding in the basements of residential homes and businesses and water being over roads in some places. In addition, manhole covers were blown off by the pressure of water flooding the city's storm sewer system. The heavy rain caused a significant amount of water damage throughout the main part of the city.

Several boaters who were unfamiliar with Fort Peck Lake were in boats that were either capsized or close to capsizing.  Valley County Search and Rescue had to respond to four calls of boats that were either in danger of sinking or which were actually swamped by the strong waves that resulted with the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of gusty winds created havoc with boaters on Fort Peck Lake. Five different incidents occurred including a swamped and capsized boat near Duck Creek. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance moves east just north of a frontal boundary that helped to trigger severe weather. The severe weather that included one of the strongest tornadoes in Montana history started in far southern Saskatchewan and moved southeast across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the deadliest tornado in Montana since 1923, and only the fourth EF-3 on record. The supercell thunderstorm developed strong rotation, and rotating wall clouds and brief funnel clouds were reported in Daniels County.  As the storm moved southeast, it intensified and a large, long-lived tornado formed.  The tornado traveled for 18 miles, mostly through rural areas, but it had devastating impacts as it hit a ranch, killing two people and injuring one.   On the morning of July 27th, 2010 two teams of NWS Glasgow staff surveyed the damage area.  The first team started in Daniels County and followed the track of the storm to the east-southeast.   The second team met with county and state officials at the site of the ranch that was most devastated.   Both teams spoke to law enforcement officials, as well as weather spotters and the public who had reports of weather and/or damage as well as those who saw the tornado.   ||The initial damage from the tornado originated about 12 miles south of Redstone.   Along the Reserve and Wanso Roads, six miles of power lines were snapped off.  Sheridan Electric was able to restore power fairly quickly to the majority of residences by the next morning.  Due to the poles being snapped, the tornado was rated an EF2 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale in this area.   This was the widest portion of the tornado track with a width of a half mile.||The tornado continued east-southeast where it encountered an abandoned farmhouse, a barn and a bridge that went across Wolf Creek on the Three Corners Road.  The bridge had the planks ripped off with damage totals coming to $100,000.  The farmhouse was blown off of the foundation, had some of the siding stripped off, and roof damage.  The barn was completely destroyed, with the west and north facing walls blown away, and the south and east facing walls intact for the most part, but flattened on the ground.  Two grain bins were destroyed, with a third still on its foundation but crushed.  They appeared to be empty.   The damage at this location was rated an EF2 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale.  The width of the tornado at this point was a quarter mile wide. ||The storm continued on its east-southeast track at a speed of about 30 mph.  The Smith Ranch was then impacted.  The ranch sits on a bit of a hill with a small stream valley from the west-northwest to just north of the property.  There are no shelter belts or terrain to block some of the winds from the tornado.  The two fatalities and one injury occurred at the Smith Ranch.  The adult fatality was trying to get into the basement.  The child fatality was in the basement and the chimney collapsed and crushed him.  The adult injury was with the child, but on the other side of the chimney in the basement. ||The tornado was rated an EF3 at the Smith Ranch, with a width of three tenths of a mile.  This was the highest rating during the life cycle of the tornado.  Along with the devastating destruction of property, there was also up to 40 cows and calves significantly injured or killed by the tornado and/or debris.  Many had severe injuries and had to be put down by a veterinarian.  ||The damage totals are estimated.   ||A formal written report is available from the NWS Glasgow office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Meteorologically speaking, this was a very active severe weather day for south central Montana. A very moist and unstable atmosphere was in place across portions of the Billings Forecast area during the afternoon and evening of the 20th (Father's Day). A moist, southeast surface flow, strong wind shear aloft, and ample afternoon heating provided the necessary ingredients for severe weather. Numerous thunderstorms, some of which became rapidly severe producing tornadoes and large hail, developed across South Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Carter County sheriff reported flooding over Hammond Road and along Mile Marker 30 on Highway 323. The Department of Emergency Services and the sheriff reported that 1-4 inches of rain fell over Central Carter County and that Box Elder Creek was running high.|In addition, a county official reported 2.5 inches of rain had fallen in one hour which resulted in flash flooding on Cabin Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A macroburst occurred where damage consisted of one doublewide manufactured home with the roof blown off, one empty storage building blown apart, and about 3 miles of downed power lines (approximately 44 poles all together) starting near mile marker 10 north of Nashua on the Nashua Road (State Highway 438). The power poles were snapped cleanly, mostly at ground level. As the poles were around 10 years old and relatively strong looking, and there were 3 miles of snapped poles, the NWS officials estimated the winds to be around 120 mph. The house and storage building had less wind, since nearby poles were not snapped at those locations. This also was the origination point to where a combined swath of hail and wind damaged 70 to 100 percent of the crops east southeast to the Roosevelt County border. This storm continued into extreme southwest Roosevelt County.||According to the USDA Hazard Risk Assessment indemnity figures, approximately $750,000 worth of crop damage occurred with this storm (as of October 12, 2010).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in the Hell Creek Recreation Area reported strong winds ahead of the thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed north of Wolf Point (Roosevelt County) and marched southeast into Richland County. Hail the size of tennis balls and damaging winds occurred in central Roosevelt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated wind gust of 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1925 MST in Terry, thunderstorm wind gusts broke large tree limbs and was accompanied by heavy rain. At 1950 MST in Fallon, thunderstorm wind gusts also broke more tree limbs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter 15 miles south of Wibaux at 1952 MST, estimated thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph. Spotter in Wibaux at 2035 MST estimated thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability and increasing flow aloft combined with an upper low and moisture over north central Oregon produced favorable conditions for severe thunderstorms across the northern Rockies. The main threat with these storms was for large hail, ranging in size from 0.75 inches in diameter up to 1.75 inches in diameter.  Heavy rain also accompanied these storms.

Severe thunderstorms developed north of Wolf Point (Roosevelt County) and marched southeast into Richland County. Hail the size of tennis balls and damaging winds occurred in central Roosevelt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed with nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough swinging through Oregon produced unstable conditions ahead of it favorable for thunderstorm development across southwest Montana. The majority of storms were not severe but one cell was able to produce one inch diameter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fencing company in Avon reported one inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance ejecting northeast across the area produced severe thunderstorms beginning across near Fort Peck lake that tracked east across McCone county and stretched north-south as it approached the North Dakota border. A weak tornado possibly touched down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew flagpole and lawn furniture away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather continued into the third week of June across the Billings Forecast Area. |Plenty of low-level moisture continued to be in place across Southeast Montana. Strong winds aloft along, an upper level disturbance and low-level moisture all combined to help produce numerous thunderstorms across Southeast Montana. A tornado watch was issued for the afternoon and evening hours. Numerous severe thunderstorms developed by mid afternoon and continued well into the evening hours. A couple thunderstorms produced tornadoes, one of which was the Cache Creek Tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cache Creek Tornado was 100 yards wide and on the ground for approximately a half mile. The tornado lasted just over ten minutes. Damage consisted of a house roof and front porch blown off, broken windows, as well as the house being shifted off of its foundation. Winds were estimated at 111-135 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed north of Wolf Point (Roosevelt County) and marched southeast into Richland County. Hail the size of tennis balls and damaging winds occurred in central Roosevelt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter observed hail ranging in size from small marbles to golfball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe thunderstorms mainly affected areas from Opheim into western Daniels County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail combined with strong winds destroyed 600 acres of wheat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass allowed thunderstorms to develop over portions of southwest Montana late in the afternoon. Hail the size of golf balls was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped 1.5 inch diameter hail at Gallatin Gateway 10S.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure circulation moving through central Idaho brought a moist and unstable airmass to western Montana.  Thunderstorms capable of producing hail and heavy rain in a short period of time were reported.

An upper level ridge moved east of Montana during the day bringing southwest flow aloft across eastern Montana. Dewpoints started off low, however a boundary of higher moisture at the surface moved across northeast Montana which became the focus of the intensified convection. The storms began in northern Blaine County then into Phillips County. Better instability allowed the strong storms to become severe as they moved east.
A thunderstorm producing severe wind gusts moved through Stillwater and Yellowstone Counties during the late afternoon hours on the 6th.
A couple of isolated thunderstorms producing severe wind gusts moved across Park and Big Horn Counties.

Some of the 2ft sheets of metal from the sign were not found. HRH Hospital light pole signs were damaged. About 1.5 miles east on I-94 another large green route sign was snapped in half and 2ft sheets of metal from the sign were blown across the interstate and into a fence. In addition, about 1/4 mile SSE of exit 141, a power pole was snapped about midway-up and appeared to be suspended in the wire. A distribution and transmission pole just outside of Miles City were reported to be broken. Five miles further east on Curcher Road, an approximate 80ft tree with a diameter of around 5ft was uprooted and fell across a ditch crushing a fence. High winds damaged several businesses and residences and brought down numerous large trees. A roof was taken off part of a home just east of Miles City along Highway 12 as well. Debris from a building on top of the plant was scattered all the way across a neighboring pasture. In addition, several trailers were damaged at Big Sky Campground.
